assignment_description:
A Sense of Fall
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Have you ever gone on a walk with a naturalist or park ranger? If so, what was that like? These are people who spend a LOT of time getting to know nature. This book has been written especially for YOU by a naturalist! Today, she is going to introduce herself and help us start to think about the upcoming season. She'll take us on her walks all year long!

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.xi-3 "Hello, readers!" - "go outside!"
Read the definitions and notes at the end of each reading, as desired.

• When you think about the fall season, what do you think of? What do you look forward to? Have you noticed any of these signs of fall yet?

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL
∞ Video Link: Fall Colors in Texas (2:08)
∞ Video Link: Wood Thrush Song (1:35)
∞ Video Link: Senses Nature Walk (6:34)

• OUTDOOR WORK
Weekly walks and daily outdoor time are vital! Resources in Quick Links.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Supplemental links for optional support are provided at the END of the lesson. They can be used at any point to help generate interest during the introduction, to enliven the lesson itself, or to add to discussion later. They are NOT required and should NOT take away from the narration and discussion. Experiment and see what works best for your student(s).

assignment_description:
Shake a Bush
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Look at the picture on p.4. Have you ever seen a creature like this before? What kinds of 'bugs' have you noticed? Briefly share in word or picture.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.4-6 "One of my" - "you caught them."

• What do you think makes a leafhopper a leafhopper? What does it do? How is it made?

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL

assignment_description:
Ants in My Plants
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Draw a picture or diagram of an ant or other insect (or a partner might diagram alongside a verbal drawing). What parts do you recall?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.8-11 "In 'Shake'" - "and differences!"

• Compare the ant on p.9 to the leafhopper on p.5. How are they similar? How are they different?
• Add to the drawing you did before the reading or draw a new one. Can you remember the parts of an insect? (3 body segments, 6 legs)
• Have you noticed insects outside? What were they doing? Where were they? What made that place a good home?

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL
∞ Video Link: All the Life Stages of an Ant (1:34)
∞ Video Link: Flying Ants Up Close (5:22)

• OUTDOOR WORK
Weekly walks and daily outdoor time are vital! Resources in Quick Links.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
If students need support drawing or diagramming, the teacher may do any activities with them in tandem or act as their scribe.

assignment_description:
Splooting and Anting
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

➜ INTRO
Recall what we read. Tell about or draw/diagram an insect. What parts do you recall? Ants live with other creatures in nature. Do you think you would like to live with ants? Why or why not? What do you think the bird is doing in the picture on p.13?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.13-15 "I love" - "around them."

• Have you ever noticed animals doing funny or curious things?

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL

∞ Video Link: Birds Bathing on an Ant Hill (2:22)

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Tell about our last reading. What do you remember about insects? Briefly share in word or picture.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.16-19 "When Mike" - "live in?"

assignment_description:
Counting Bumbles
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
What do you remember about our last reading? Look at the picture on p.20. Do you know who that is? We're going to read about another insect today and find out why he's in that tube. If it's an insect, what body parts does that mean it will have? (6 legs and 3 body segments: head, thorax, abdomen)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.20-24 "Did you know" - "nature journal."

• Bumble bees are insects just like ants and leafhoppers. Looking at the picture on p.22, how is the bumble bee different from ants and leafhoppers?

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL
∞ Video Link: Bumble bees in slow motion (watch as much as you want, trying to notice the legs, body segments, and any other interesting features or behaviors)

assignment_description:
Scary Spider? No Way!
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Tell me what we've been reading about. Look at the picture on p.25. Does this creature have three body segments and six legs, like the ant and bumble bee? How many body segments and how many legs does the spider have? Spiders are not insects! Have you ever watched a spider or seen a spider web before? Tell about it.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.25-28 "I used to be" - "look like?"

• What do you want to notice about the next spider web you find? Where do you think an orb weaver would make its home?

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL

∞ Video Link: Amazing Spider with Huge Web (3:15)

assignment_description:
Cute Spiders?
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Remind me what we read about last. How was the garden spider we met last week different from the insects we met earlier in the book? Let's meet another spider today. Compare the pictures on p.27, 30. How are the spiders similar? How are they different?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.30-33 "Do you think" - "your mind!"

• Did you notice how some jumping spiders make their home in a leaf? Where do you think they might live outside? That place where they find food and shelter is their habitat!

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL
∞ Video Link: Spider with Super Powers (4:00)

• OUTDOOR WORK
Weekly walks and daily outdoor time are vital! Resources in Quick Links.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER NOTE
The spider in the supplemental video catches prey, which some students might find disturbing. Teachers should preview or skip this one if they have concerns.

assignment_description:
Chimney Swift Tornado
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

➜ INTRO
What are some of the creatures we've read about this term? Where do they live and find habitat (a place with food and shelter) in the world around us? Do they ever come into your home? Do we share the same habitat as insects and spiders or are their homes separate from ours? Today we'll read about a bird whose habitat is definitely part of someone else's! Look at the picture on p.34.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.34-36 "One evening" - "they roost?"

• If our homes are part of the natural world, then the creatures in nature are our neighbors! Tell how you care for your neighbors, human or animal.

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL
∞ Video Link: Chimney Swifts (0:45)
∞ Video Link: Portland Swift Tornado (watch as much as you like)

• OUTDOOR WORK
Weekly walks and daily outdoor time are vital! Resources in Quick Links.

Yard Elk
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

➜ INTRO
Tell what you remember about the chimney swift. Have you noticed any bird neighbors this week? What were they doing? Have you noticed signs that they may be getting ready for a change in season? Today our naturalist friend tells us about another animal neighbor that she is getting to know.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.38-40 "Today, a herd" - "for winter?"

• How do you think animals that live outside all year round survive in very cold seasons?

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL
∞ Video Link: Elk in the Ozarks (2:19)
∞ Video Link: Bighorn Sheep, another ruminant in a different habitat (3:20)
∞ Video Link: White-tailed Deer, another ruminant in a different habitat (2:32)

• OUTDOOR WORK
Weekly walks and daily outdoor time are vital! Resources in Quick Links.

Catch Up

➜ CATCH UP
Use this time to catch up on lessons, watch some videos that you didn't have time for, or discuss some interesting ideas over a snack. Depending on your discussions, these MIGHT include:
• What do you remember about ants?
• How are bees similar to and different from ants?
• How are spiders different from insects?
• Other creatures as our neighbors.
• Signs of the season with all our senses and signs that animals are preparing for that season.

assignment_description:
Sticky Seeds
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Look at the picture on p.41. Did you ever find stuff stuck to you after you went out to play or walk? What did it look like? Where did it come from?

p.41-42 "Sometimes Mike" - "closer look."
Read the notes and definitions at the end of each reading, as desired.

• Have you ever seen the seeds of a wild plant? If so, briefly tell about it.
• Tell a story about where a seed goes and how it finds its new home after it sticks to an animal.

assignment_description:
Pinecone Bombs
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

PREP: Read Teacher Tip

➜ INTRO
Remind me about the seeds we read about last week. Look at the picture on p.44. What do you suppose the squirrel is doing on that branch?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.44-46 "Mike and I" - "seeds inside."

• Last week we met a plant that helps its seeds move by making them sticky. How did this week's plant help its seeds move? (Might mention animals eating or burying, wind). Can you think of other ways that seeds move around to find a good home? How is the seed helped by the way it was made?

assignment_description:
The Bird with the Best Memory
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

➜ INTRO
Let's remember the seeds we've read about so far and how they move around. Today we're going to read about a different kind of pine seed and an animal that helps this plant move around.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.48-50 "One fall morning" - "your dinner!"

• Have you noticed a bird stuffing its mouth or flying busily back and forth at the bird feeder? Maybe it was caching! Act like the bird and show what it did.
• A keystone species is a creature that many other creatures rely on. Have you noticed a tree that many other creatures rely on? Tell about it.

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL
∞ Video Link: Clark's Nutcracker (1:35)
∞ Video Link: American Crow preparing to cache (1:29)
∞ Website Link: All About Birds, scroll down to see some different birds that cache and the different strategies they use

• OUTDOOR WORK
Weekly walks and daily outdoor time are vital! Resources in Quick Links.

A Bear-y Nice Tree
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

➜ INTRO
What kinds of seeds have we been reading about? Tell me about the animals that like to eat seeds and fruits. Today, let's read about one that Melissa observed in her yard.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.51-53 "Mike and I" - "eat the fruit!"

• What does the seed do for the plant? What does the seed do for the animals?

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.55-59 "It was a" - "of their own!"

assignment_description:
Christmas Bird Count Part 2
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

➜ INTRO
What did we read about last? Did you see birds this week? Did you hear birds this week? Let's find out what other birds Melissa noticed on her Count.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.63-66 "On our Christmas" - "can you find?"

• Is there a bird song you hear often? Can you sing that song? Have you seen who it belongs to? Where do you hear it?

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL

Flowers to Seeds
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

➜ INTRO
Remind me of what we read about last time. What do birds eat during the winter? Look at the picture on p.67. What is that bird doing?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.67-70 "Our yard" - "a winter meal?"

• Tell about a place where you saw wildflowers in the summer or fall. Have you noticed who is eating at that flower in winter?

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL

The Round House
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

➜ INTRO
Tell me what we read about last week. Look at the picture on p.71. What do you notice? What do you think is going on in the picture?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.71-72 "I am" - "goldenrod gall fly!"

• Have you ever thought of what it would be like to be an insect? Tell a story about an insect you've seen from the insect's point-of-view.

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL
∞ Video Link: What Gall! (2:58)

Weekly walks and daily outdoor time are vital! Resources in Quick Links.

The Goldenrod Gall Fly
⍞ Materials: Website link

➜ INTRO
What did we read about last? Look at the picture on p.74. What do you notice about this insect?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.74-78 "Mike and I" - "find a gall!"

• Galls are just one way that some insects raise their babies. If you have ever seen a wasp nest or bees emerging from the ground, then you've seen other homes for baby insects. Tell about any kinds of insect homes that you have seen.
• We often don't see many insects in the colder season, but if you watched either of the supplemental videos, can you see the parts that make these creatures insects? (3 body segments, 6 legs)

➜ SUPPLEMENTAL

• Tell about the different kinds of animal homes you can think of.

Spring Babies
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

➜ INTRO
What do you remember about buds opening from the last reading? Baby animals also emerge in the spring. Have you ever watched a baby animal?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.83-85 "In Yellowstone" - "on a farm."

A Yard Bouquet

➜ INTRO
Tell me about the signs of new life that we've been reading about (buds and baby animals). New flowers are another sign of spring. Have you ever made a bouquet of wildflowers? Tell about your favorite wildflowers. Look at the picture of Melissa's favorite wildflowers on p.87. Do you know any of them?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.87-91 "In spring" - "look closely?"

Rolling Logs
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

➜ INTRO
Remind me of what we read about last time. Have you ever thought of a dead tree as a sign of life? When you've been outside, have you ever come across a log on the ground? If so, tell what you noticed. Maybe you noticed someone like in the picture on p.92. What do you notice about this creature?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.92-96 "In our North" - "where you live!"
Be sure to read the safety note on p.97.

assignment_description:
Spider Drums
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

➜ INTRO
Tell me who we met last week. Can you remember back to the spiders we've read about? (garden/orb weaver, jumping spider) Let's meet another spider this week because spiders are busy in the spring, too.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.106-107 "One spring" - "that sound?"

• What makes spiders different from the insects, such as the tree hoppers and ants? (Help students remember that insects have 3 body segments and 6 legs, while spiders have 2 body segments and 8 legs.)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.108-110 "I was born" - "also amazing?"

• Do you know if you have annual or periodical cicadas where you live? Have you noticed their exuvia on your walks?

assignment_description:
Sneaky Spider
⍞ Materials: Walks with a Naturalist 1

➜ INTRO
Tell me about the insects we've been reading about. Do you recall who we met before them? Today, we meet another spider.
